author: motorsportcom

caption:
PSG vs Bayern Munich 

.
.
.
Tonight’s 5-4 thriller mirrored the high-stakes intensity of Verstappen vs. Hamilton in 2021, with both heavyweights trading blows until the final second. Much like that iconic F1 title race, the match was a chaotic, wheel-to-wheel battle where PSG looked to have pulled away, only for Bayern to hunt them down and close the gap in the final laps. It was a "Max vs. Lewis" moment for football—total aggression, no room for error, and a result that leaves the championship decided by the thinnest of margins heading into the final showdown.
#formula1 #ucl #psg #bayernmünchen #f1memes

caption:
Ferrari almost every season 

#formula1 #f1memes #scuderiaferrari #f1reels 

Since their last World Constructors’ Championship in 2008, Scuderia Ferrari has established a reputation for remarkable but frustrating consistency, frequently finishing in second place.

Over the last two decades, the team has secured the runner-up spot in the Constructors’ Championship at least seven times—specifically in 2012, 2015, 2017, 2018, 2019, 2022, 2024. This trend extends to their drivers; legends like Fernando Alonso (2010, 2012, 2013), Sebastian Vettel (2017, 2018), and Charles Leclerc (2022) have all narrowly missed the title while wearing the scarlet red.
author: f1troll

caption:
F1 fans after the Japanese GP

#formula1 #f1memes #f1reels #fyp 
At the Japanese GP, Mercedes, McLaren, and Ferrari fans are ecstatic, celebrating a “return to form” as all three iconic teams shared the podium in a show of pure competitive revival. There’s a collective sense of relief and high energy, with supporters feeling their teams have finally mastered the 2026 regulations.

In contrast, Red Bull fans are devastated, watching their team tumble to a distant 8th place. The mood is one of shock and frustration, as the “Verstappen era” of dominance appears to have vanished, replaced by deep concerns over the team’s struggling new power unit.
author: f1troll

caption:
Everyone happy except Mercedes fans

.
.
From Suzuka perfection in 2025 to setting the tone for 2026 — Max Verstappen proving that dominance isn’t a moment, it’s a mindset. 🏁

At the 2025 Japanese Grand Prix, Max Verstappen delivered one of his most complete performances. He secured pole position with an almost flawless qualifying lap, extracting maximum performance from the car through Suzuka’s demanding high-speed corners. From the race start, he maintained control at the front, managing pace, tyres, and pressure without making any significant errors, even with strong competition close behind.

What made Verstappen stand out was his precision and consistency throughout the weekend. At a circuit where even the smallest mistake can cost time, he drove with total confidence, especially through the technical first sector. By converting pole into victory, he demonstrated not just speed but race intelligence and composure, reinforcing his reputation as one of the most dominant and reliable drivers on the grid
#formula1 #f1memes #f1 #racing

caption:
Our season starts this week! 
Join us on Patreon on Tuesday, 3rd March, for a close look at all the details in our 2026 liveries. 

And on Youtube on Wednesday, 4th March for our season opener episode. (It’s the longest video we’ve ever made(/making, still working on it).

This marks the first major evolutionary upgrade for our characters since 2020. Excited for a new season!
